header.service: DEMO — UC San Diego Staff Performance Appraisal (SPA)
header.scope: Setting expectations → Signed appraisal filed in personnel file
header.others: People leader (supervisor); HR / SPA administrators; department business officer

doing.value: Meets with their leader early in the cycle to review the job description and agree on standards and goals.

wants.value: To know what good looks like for the year ahead, in concrete terms.

touchpoints.value: Goal-setting meeting; SPA system; the job description record.

thinkingFeeling.value: "Tell me what actually matters this year so I'm not guessing in April."

waitingFor.value: A meeting to be scheduled; an updated job description.

effortWhy.value: Low when the meeting happens; the burden is on the leader to initiate.

stages.name: Doing the work and gathering evidence

doing.value: Does the year's work; informally collects accomplishments and feedback as they go.

wants.value: To not lose track of what they did so it counts at review time.

touchpoints.value: Day-to-day systems; personal notes; any mid-year check-ins.

thinkingFeeling.value: "I'll remember the big stuff" — but often won't by spring.

waitingFor.value: Nothing formal; the cycle runs in the background.

effortWhy.value: Low day to day, but the lack of structure shows up later as reconstruction effort.

stages.name: Writing my self-appraisal

doing.value: Completes the (optional but encouraged) self-appraisal in SPA and submits it to the leader.

wants.value: To make sure their leader remembers the full year, not just recent months.

touchpoints.value: SPA self-appraisal form; reminder email; their own brag-doc workaround.

thinkingFeeling.value: "It's optional, so is it even worth it?" — many skip it.

waitingFor.value: A clear deadline from the leader.

effortWhy.value: Moderate: reconstructing a year of work from scattered notes takes real time.

stages.duration: 2–4 hours, 30–60 days before the formal meeting

stages.name: Getting the draft and talking it through

doing.value: Receives the leader's draft appraisal and discusses it in the appraisal meeting.

wants.value: A fair reflection of the year and a conversation, not a verdict handed down.

touchpoints.value: Appraisal meeting; SPA draft (Model A or B).

thinkingFeeling.value: "Did they actually see my year, or write this in the last-minute rush?"

waitingFor.value: The leader to finish the draft and book the meeting.

effortWhy.value: Moderate emotional effort; the conversation is high-stakes because it feeds merit.

stages.duration: 45–60 minute meeting; draft timing varies with leader workload

stages.name: Review, comments, and HR clearance

doing.value: Adds employee comments to the appraisal, then waits as it moves to 'HR Review' where they can no longer edit or see it.

wants.value: Enough time to respond, and to know where their appraisal stands.

touchpoints.value: SPA comment field; status changes they can't see; email.

thinkingFeeling.value: "It went into a black box and nobody can tell me when it'll come out."

waitingFor.value: At least five business days to comment (sometimes less); HR clearance.

effortWhy.value: High: opacity and time pressure combine right when the stakes are highest.

stages.duration: ≥5 business days to comment; HR clearance days to weeks during the crunch

stages.name: Signing and closing

doing.value: Signs the final appraisal, receives a copy, and the signed document is filed in their personnel file.

wants.value: To be done, with a copy they can keep and a clear link to merit decisions.

thinkingFeeling.value: Relief, usually — though chasing signatures drags the close out.

waitingFor.value: All required signatures to be collected and routed.

effortWhy.value: Moderate: the content is settled, but signature logistics stall the finish near the June 15 deadline.

stages.duration: Days; concentrated against the COB June 15 deadline

momentsThatMatter.moment: The appraisal conversation and the rating (stage 4)
momentsThatMatter.why: It is what the employee remembers and what feeds merit-increase decisions.
It largely determines how complete and fair the draft can be.

dropoutPoints.what: Because it is optional, many employees skip it; their leader then drafts from memory.
The employee loses visibility entirely and routes around it by emailing their leader for status.
